sentences.base_language: I wake up at half past seven.
sentences.target_language: Me despierto a las siete y media.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Me despierto** — **despertarse** is a reflexive e→ie stem-changing verb: **me despierto, te despiertas, se despierta, nos despertamos, os despertáis, se despiertan**.
The stem changes in all forms except nosotros/vosotros.
**a las siete y media** (at half past seven) — time expressions use **a las** for all hours except one o'clock (**a la una**).

sentences.base_language: I stretch and get up slowly.
sentences.target_language: Me estiro y me levanto despacio.
sentences.grammarNote:
Both verbs are reflexive: **estirarse** (to stretch oneself) · **levantarse** (to get up).
Reflexive pronouns **me** attach before the conjugated verb.
**levantarse** is regular -ar; **estirarse** is also regular -ar. Both use **me** for the first person singular.
**despacio** (slowly) — invariable adverb.

sentences.base_language: I wash my face with cold water.
sentences.target_language: Me lavo la cara con agua fría.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Me lavo la cara** — reflexive **lavarse** with **definite article** instead of possessive: not *mi cara*.
The reflexive pronoun **me** already indicates whose face is being washed.
This is the standard pattern for all body-care reflexive verbs: **me lavo las manos**, **me cepillo los dientes**, **me peino el pelo**.
**con agua fría** — note **fría** (feminine) agrees with **agua** which is grammatically feminine (despite using **el** when preceded by article).

sentences.base_language: I brush my teeth for two minutes.
sentences.target_language: Me cepillo los dientes durante dos minutos.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Durante' = during / for (a duration): 'durante dos minutos' (for two minutes), 'durante la semana' (during the week). Compare 'por', which also means 'for' with time but feels less specific: 'trabajé por dos horas' (I worked for about two hours).

With body-care reflexive verbs, use the definite article instead of a possessive adjective: 'me peino el pelo' (I comb my hair), 'me lavo las manos' (I wash my hands). The reflexive pronoun (me/te/se) already shows whose body part it is — no need to say 'mi pelo' or 'mis manos'.

**vestirse** (to get dressed) is a reflexive e→i stem-changing verb: **me visto, te vistes, se viste, nos vestimos, os vestís, se visten**.
Note the stem vowel **e→i** in all forms except nosotros/vosotros — different from the e→ie pattern.
Other e→i verbs: **pedir** (pido), **servir** (sirvo), **repetir** (repito).
**Luego** (then/later) — a common sequencing adverb: **primero... luego... después...**

sentences.base_language: I look at myself in the mirror.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Me miro** — **mirarse** (to look at oneself, reflexive): the **me** shows the action reflects back on the subject.
Contrast: **miro el espejo** (I look at the mirror — the mirror is the object) vs. **me miro en el espejo** (I look at myself in the mirror — I am both subject and object).
**el espejo** (mirror) — from Latin **speculum**: related to 'inspect,' 'speculate,' 'spectacle.'

'Listo' with ser means clever/smart: 'Es muy listo' (He's very smart). With estar, it means ready: 'Estoy listo' (I'm ready). This ser/estar contrast — where the same adjective shifts meaning — also applies to: aburrido (ser: boring / estar: bored), malo (ser: bad/evil / estar: sick), bueno (ser: good / estar: tasty/feeling well).
